sentences.base_language: Tomorrow, Rodrigo has to give a presentation in front of the whole company.
sentences.target_language: Mañana, Rodrigo tiene que dar una presentación frente a toda la empresa.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Tener que + infinitive** = to have to do something — one of the most common obligation expressions.
**Dar una presentación** = to give a presentation — **dar** (irregular yo: **doy**) used with many event nouns: **dar un discurso** (give a speech), **dar una charla** (give a talk).
**Frente a** = in front of — a preposition phrase; compare **delante de** (also *in front of*, more physical).

sentences.base_language: Tonight he can't sleep. He's very nervous.
sentences.target_language: Esta noche no puede dormir. Está muy nervioso.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Estar nervioso** uses estar, not ser — it describes a temporary emotional state.
ser tranquilo = to be a calm person by nature
estar nervioso = to be nervous right now
With most emotions, use estar: estar feliz, estar triste, estar emocionado.

sentences.base_language: His friend Laura calls him. 'Hey, how are you feeling?'
sentences.target_language: Su amiga Laura lo llama. —Oye, ¿cómo te sientes?
sentences.grammarNote:
**Lo llama** = calls him — **lo** is the direct object pronoun (masculine, *him*).
**¿Cómo te sientes?** = How are you feeling? — **sentirse** (reflexive): **me siento, te sientes, se siente**.
Contrast with **¿cómo estás?** (how are you? — general) — **¿cómo te sientes?** asks more specifically about your inner emotional state.

sentences.base_language: 'Honestly? I have butterflies in my stomach. What if I blank out?'
sentences.target_language: —¿La verdad? Tengo mariposas en el estómago. ¿Y si me quedo en blanco?
sentences.grammarNote:
**Tener mariposas en el estómago** = to have butterflies in your stomach — works the same as in English.
**¿Y si...?** = What if...? Great for expressing worry about hypotheticals: ¿Y si llego tarde? ¿Y si no saben la respuesta?

sentences.base_language: 'You're not going to blank out. You've prepared a lot for this.'
sentences.target_language: —No te vas a quedar en blanco. Has preparado mucho para esto.
sentences.grammarNote:
**No te vas a quedar en blanco** = You're not going to blank out — **ir a + infinitive** for near future: **te vas a quedar** (you're going to stay/end up).
**Quedarse en blanco** = to blank out (reflexive) — literally *to go blank*.
**Has preparado** = you have prepared — present perfect: **haber** (present) + past participle **preparado**.

sentences.base_language: 'I know, but I'm terrified of speaking in public.'
sentences.target_language: —Ya sé, pero me aterra hablar en público.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Ya sé** = I know (already) — **ya** + present tense can mean *already* or emphasize current state: **ya sé** (I already know / I know, I know).
**Me aterra** = it terrifies me — **aterrar** works like **gustar**: the terrifying thing is the subject (**hablar en público**).
**Hablar en público** = to speak in public — infinitive as subject of the clause.

sentences.base_language: 'Everyone gets nervous. The important thing is that you breathe and speak slowly.'
sentences.target_language: —Todo el mundo se pone nervioso. Lo importante es que respires y hables despacio.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Lo importante es que + subjunctive** — expresses what someone should do (not a stated fact). The subjunctive is triggered by the recommendation:
lo importante es que respires y hables despacio.
Other examples: lo bueno es que vengas, lo importante es que practiques.

sentences.base_language: 'What if they ask me something I don't know?'
sentences.target_language: —¿Y si me preguntan algo que no sé?
sentences.grammarNote:
**¿Y si + present indicative?** = What if...? — used to raise a worry about something that might happen.
**Me preguntan** = they ask me — **me** is the indirect object (they ask me something).
**Algo que no sé** = something (that) I don't know — relative clause with **que**; the relative pronoun can't be dropped in Spanish (unlike English).

sentences.base_language: 'Then you say "that's a great question, let me look into that." Relax!'
sentences.target_language: —Pues dices «qué buena pregunta, lo voy a investigar». ¡Tranquilízate!
sentences.grammarNote:
**Pues** = well, then — a filler/connector meaning *so* or *well then*.
**Lo voy a investigar** = I'm going to look into that — **ir a + infinitive** for near future; **lo** is the direct object (that/it).
**Tranquilízate** = Calm down! / Relax! — reflexive tú command of **tranquilizarse**: drop the **-s** from **te tranquilizas**, add **-te**: **tranquilízate** (accent preserves stress).

sentences.base_language: Rodrigo laughs. He feels a little better.
sentences.target_language: Rodrigo se ríe. Se siente un poco mejor.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Se ríe** = he laughs — reflexive form of **reír**: **reírse** (essentially interchangeable with **reír** in many contexts).
**Se siente un poco mejor** = he feels a little better — **sentirse** (reflexive) + **un poco** (a little) + **mejor** (better — comparative of **bien**).

sentences.base_language: 'You're right. I'm going to do great. I just need to sleep.'
sentences.target_language: —Tienes razón. Lo voy a hacer bien. Solo necesito dormir.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Tienes razón** = You're right — **tener razón** is a **tener + noun** expression (literally *to have reason*).
**Lo voy a hacer bien** = I'm going to do it well — **lo** is a direct object pronoun anticipating *it* (the presentation).
**Bien** vs **bueno**: **lo voy a hacer bien** (I'll do it well — adverb) vs **lo voy a hacer bueno** would be unusual; **bien** modifies the action.

sentences.base_language: 'Exactly. Get some rest. You've got this!'
sentences.grammarNote:
**Descansa** = Get some rest / Rest — affirmative tú command of **descansar** (regular -ar verb: drop **-s** from present tense **descansas**).
**Tú puedes con esto** = You've got this / You can handle this — **poder con** = to be able to handle/cope with: **puedo con todo** (I can handle everything).
**Tú** is written with accent to distinguish from **tu** (your — possessive).
